Drawing on a story about Ajahn Chah and the Thai forest tradition, he warns against ego inflation in teaching, then turns to sila, feedback, and communal safety as practical protections. The talk moves toward a quiet but forceful conclusion: practice can deepen our power, but it should also make us safer, more transparent, and more able to serve as a refuge for others.
short_summary: A reflective talk on how Dharma practice increases power while making humility, ethical conduct, and communal safety more important.

chapters.title: Why Power Matters
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the talk by naming a context of harm and unethical leadership, then frames the question of ethical conduct.

chapters.title: Ajahn Chah's Warning
chapters.description: He relays Kittisaro's account of Ajahn Chah advising monks not to identify as teachers, but to remain practitioners first.

chapters.title: Ripples of Goodness
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on how practice and teaching spread across time, and how one moment of goodness can reverberate for decades.

chapters.title: Dharma as Response
chapters.description: He turns to the Thai forest custom of requesting a Dhamma talk, using it to show that teaching should answer a real need rather than become performance.

chapters.title: Unpossessable Dharma
chapters.description: The talk develops the theme that Dharma, goodness, and even our roles cannot be owned, and that surrender and softness are necessary.

chapters.title: Feedback and Safety
chapters.description: Matthew emphasizes that we cannot fully see ourselves, so honest feedback and shared calibration are essential for communal safety.

chapters.title: Power and Prestige
chapters.description: He returns to the language of power and prestige, arguing that one cannot perform Dharma but must become it in a way that remains safe and ethical.

chapters.title: A Quiet Trace
chapters.description: The talk closes by linking insight into emptiness with safety, then asking listeners how they will use their power and make their hearts a refuge.

title: Dharmette: Courage and Fear
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on courage as something distinct from general effort, and as a practice that is often shadowed by fear. He traces how fear narrows attention, distorts cognition, and makes letting go feel unsafe, then turns toward the way spiritual practice asks for honesty, surrender, and the willingness to meet uncertainty. The talk closes by connecting courage to virtue, the Buddhist perfections, and a quiet question about what love looks like when it is less afraid.
short_summary: A grounded reflection on how fear shapes the mind, and why courage is needed for letting go, self-honesty, and moral bravery.

chapters.title: Opening to courage
chapters.description: Matthew introduces courage as the day’s theme and speaks candidly about his own temperament and limited confidence teaching on it.

chapters.title: Fear at the threshold
chapters.description: He describes courage as disorienting because it asks us to step toward what we cannot fully predict and to accept possible pain or change.

chapters.title: Faith and the path
chapters.description: The talk turns to the spiritual path as a series of small acts of courage, where aspiration requires leaving old habits without knowing exactly what will replace them.

chapters.title: How fear works
chapters.description: Matthew explores fear as a deeply wired force that narrows cognition, makes clinging feel protective, and disrupts attention and samadhi.

chapters.title: Groundless moments
chapters.description: He names the courage needed to face suffering, change, and the moments when the ground feels unstable or entirely groundless.

chapters.title: Terror and waiting
chapters.description: Using the example of being lost in the woods, he suggests that sometimes the wisest response is not to force a solution but to stay still and let help arrive.

chapters.title: Fear of suffering
chapters.description: Matthew distinguishes sensitivity to suffering from fear of suffering, and notices how the wish to control pain can quietly shape practice.

chapters.title: Shared courage
chapters.description: He reflects on the courage he sees in others, invokes Aquinas on courage as foundational to virtue, and gathers the talk toward moral bravery.

chapters.title: Love less afraid
chapters.description: The closing question asks what love looks like when fear loosens, ending with a brief exhortation to keep going.

title: Dharmette: Surrender
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on surrender as a quiet but central part of practice: not resignation, but a willing release of control, habit, and the need to know. He moves from Leonard Cohen and St. Augustine into a Buddhist account of mindfulness, samadhi, and insight as forms of letting the self soften. The talk stays grounded in ordinary practice, where surrender becomes a way to meet experience honestly and find a different kind of power.
short_summary: A quiet talk on surrender as the release of control that allows mindfulness, insight, and deeper trust to mature.

chapters.title: Cohen and the search
chapters.description: Matthew opens with Leonard Cohen as an image of a life where striving gives way and even the search dissolves.

chapters.title: Restless heart
chapters.description: He turns to St. Augustine to evoke longing, humility, and the wish to be enlarged and remade by what is greater than the self.

chapters.title: Surrender in Dharma
chapters.description: Matthew reframes surrender in Buddhist terms as yielding to the moment, love, not-self, and the path rather than to a deity.

chapters.title: Discernment and trust
chapters.description: He balances surrender with the Kalama Sutta’s emphasis on verification, while warning that surrender can be distorted by bad ideas, habits, or people.

chapters.title: The will to control
chapters.description: Using Carl Rogers and his own language, he describes how practice often serves the ego until a phase shift begins and control starts to loosen.

chapters.title: Changed by experience
chapters.description: Matthew explores the point when the ego runs out of moves and practice becomes an openness to being changed by the moment and the breath.

chapters.title: Not knowing and refuge
chapters.description: He links surrender with letting go of ownership, relinquishing stories that no longer serve, and resting in groundlessness without losing dignity.

chapters.title: Gateway to absorption
chapters.description: Near the end, he describes how effort can become agitation at the threshold of samadhi and jhana, where trust in silence and the present becomes essential.

chapters.title: True power
chapters.description: Matthew closes by saying that the path still cultivates power, but the deepest power appears through surrender rather than control.

title: Dharmette: Self-Esteem Preoccupation as Symptom
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on self-esteem as a symptom rather than a solution, drawing on social psychology and Dharma to question the habit of measuring worth. He moves from scientific conversation and familiar retreat anecdotes into a quieter argument that suffering, fear, and need deprivation generate self-preoccupation, and that practice can soften that grip. The talk closes by pointing toward ordinariness, ethical living, refuge, and the possibility that it is enough simply to be a person.
short_summary: A short Dharma reflection on self-esteem, selfing, and the relief of becoming ordinary.

chapters.title: Opening the conversation
chapters.description: Matthew begins with a few light remarks about the Dharma path and its conversation with science.

chapters.title: Science and Dharma
chapters.description: He recalls a Mind and Life conference with Richard Davidson to show how scientific and contemplative approaches are learning from each other.

chapters.title: Self-esteem as symptom
chapters.description: Using Brown and Ryan, he frames self-esteem preoccupation as a sign of unmet needs rather than a healthy foundation.

chapters.title: Not-self without idealism
chapters.description: He turns to not-self as a way of seeing things as they are, not as a moral project or spiritual achievement.

chapters.title: Ordinary worthiness
chapters.description: A memory of Jack Kornfield leads into a gentler understanding of dignity rooted in ordinariness rather than specialness.

chapters.title: Selfing and pain
chapters.description: Matthew argues that selfing is an effect of pain, fear, and grasping, not a fixed cause standing apart from them.

chapters.title: Thawing the wound
chapters.description: He describes a gradual thaw in which heaviness, shame, and unmet need are met more tenderly.

chapters.title: Ethics and refuge
chapters.description: Ethical conduct and taking refuge are presented as ways life becomes less self-centered and more incidental.

chapters.title: Just being a person
chapters.description: He closes with Garfield's distinction between self and person, suggesting it is enough to play our roles without proving who we are.

title: Dharmette: Expansiveness of Awe
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on awe as a kind of pleasant disorientation that widens experience rather than narrowing it. He moves from nature and virtuosity to moral beauty, the history of the Dharma, and the spaciousness that can appear when suffering and convention are seen more lightly. The talk closes by linking awe with freedom, love, and a less claustrophobic way of living.
short_summary: A quiet reflection on awe as a widening of mind, heart, and possible ways of living.

chapters.title: Disorientation and Awe
chapters.description: He begins by reframing disorientation as something Dharma practice can acclimatize us to, then introduces awe as a pleasant but slightly unsettling widening of mind.

chapters.title: What Awe Does
chapters.description: Using Dacher Keltner’s definition, he describes awe as encounter with something vast and notes its links to generosity, nature, art, and moral elevation.

chapters.title: Nature, Skill, Goodness
chapters.description: He gives examples from mountains, Simone Biles, and ordinary goodness to show how awe can come through landscape, virtuosity, and moral beauty.

chapters.title: Beyond Claustrophobia
chapters.description: Awe is presented as a way out of the claustrophobic self, opening a broader sense of life, practice, and lineage.

chapters.title: Dharma Across Time
chapters.description: He turns to gratitude for the Dharma and recalls an early retreat experience that made the vastness of practice feel emotionally overwhelming in a good way.

chapters.title: Closeness of Peace
chapters.description: Awe becomes tied to the proximity of peace, the preciousness of human life, and the strangeness of simply being here at all.

chapters.title: Freedom and Convention
chapters.description: Drawing on existential psychotherapy and the fetter of attachment to convention, he asks how to live when so much of what feels solid is self-made.

chapters.title: Stars and Smallness
chapters.description: A retreat memory under the stars becomes a meditation on vast space and time, making personal worries feel small and life feel more open.

chapters.title: Living in Awe
chapters.description: He closes by suggesting that we do not fully acclimate to awe and that its openness can remain a living question rather than a settled state.

title: Dharmette: Grief as a Facet of Dharma
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers grief as something deeper than a passing emotion: a companion to wakefulness, love, and ethical growth. He moves through the Buddha’s life, the First Noble Truth, and contemporary psychology to suggest that Dharma practice does not erase grief so much as transmute it. The talk stays close to the lived experience of change, loss, and the widening of the heart.
short_summary: A quiet Dharma reflection on grief as an inseparable part of awakening, love, and ethical maturation.

chapters.title: Dharma and tension
chapters.description: He begins by framing Dharma as a set of teachings held in dynamic tension, introducing grief as one indispensable side of practice.

chapters.title: Grief as irreducible
chapters.description: He names grief as the space between freedom and suffering, and argues that even deep practice does not eliminate it.

chapters.title: The Buddha and loss
chapters.description: Using Leonard Cohen and the Buddha’s own life story, he shows how wakefulness and loss belong together rather than cancel each other out.

chapters.title: Not a pathology
chapters.description: He turns to anthropodenial and explains that grief is not simply an afflictive emotion to be removed, though it can become stuck or misleading.

chapters.title: Practice and growing pains
chapters.description: He links grief to grief-informed ethics, the pain of letting go, and the growing pains that come with deeper compassion and change.

chapters.title: Facing dukkha
chapters.description: Drawing on the First Noble Truth and Shelley Taylor’s work on positive illusions, he contrasts truthfulness with self-deception and the agitation it creates.

chapters.title: Grieving to change
chapters.description: James Baldwin helps him distinguish real transformation from merely expressive sorrow, emphasizing that grief can accompany genuine change.

chapters.title: Grief into love
chapters.description: He closes by describing grief as pregnant with bodhicitta, a process of purification in which pain is not far from love.

title: Dharmette: Humor in the Dharma
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a short Dharma talk on humor and playfulness as part of practice, arguing that seriousness is not the same as sincerity and that insight can make room for laughter. He moves through personal stories, references to Thai Forest teachers, and quotes from George Saunders, Freud, Nietzsche, and Winnicott to show how humor can reveal truth, loosen self-consciousness, and deepen friendship. The talk closes with an account of crying and laughing at once, suggesting that grief, joy, and peace can co-occur without canceling each other out.
short_summary: A brief Dharma talk on how humor, play, and lightness can coexist with depth, grief, and insight.

chapters.title: A playful fake list
chapters.description: He introduces the idea of a personal, unofficial list of Dharma factors, setting up humor and playfulness as the first theme.

chapters.title: Captain Buzzkill
chapters.description: A story about being called out for dampening excitement leads into his claim that Dharma can become overly serious when ego takes over.

chapters.title: Seriousness and lightness
chapters.description: He argues that practice can make experience more spacious and less self-bound, which opens the door to humor.

chapters.title: Truth told quickly
chapters.description: George Saunders and Freud are used to suggest that humor and shared laughter can reveal truth and deeper common ground.

chapters.title: Learning to play
chapters.description: Nietzsche’s idea of mature play frames Dharma as a way of recovering ease, non-vigilance, and less self-consciousness.

chapters.title: Therapy and play
chapters.description: Winnicott’s account of therapy as shared play is used to widen the point: healing often means moving from rigidity into playfulness.

chapters.title: Playing with self
chapters.description: He explores how practice can loosen the conventions of self, letting humor meet shame, pain, and defensiveness without denial.

chapters.title: Crying and laughing
chapters.description: A breathing-workshop story brings the talk to a close with crying, laughter, and peace coexisting in one porous experience.

title: Dharmette: Question about Etiquette Approaching Teachers
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver responds to a listener’s question about etiquette in approaching meditation teachers by shifting the focus from rules to relationship. He frames teaching as service and devotion, then explores projection, transference, and the ways teachers and students can use contact to see the mind more clearly. The talk closes with a careful distinction between wanting to be loved and wanting to be understood, and with sincerity as the heart of genuine Dharma exchange.
short_summary: A clear, reflective talk about how students and teachers meet in sincerity rather than etiquette or ego.

chapters.title: Question and framing
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the question about approaching teachers and uses a comic reference to open the issue of approval and need.

chapters.title: Teaching as service
chapters.description: He suggests teachers want very little from students, and describes teaching as a practice of compassion, devotion, and care for the Dharma.

chapters.title: No fixed etiquette
chapters.description: Matthew argues that teachers should meet students flexibly and create unpressured space rather than impose a code of conduct.

chapters.title: Projection and transference
chapters.description: He turns to how teacher relationships activate old patterns, including psychoanalytic ideas of projection and transference.

chapters.title: Relationship as healing
chapters.description: Matthew says these dynamics can help reveal need and longing, and that spiritual maturity includes tolerating ambivalence.

chapters.title: What teachers want
chapters.description: He identifies sincerity as the main quality teachers look for, describing it as willingness to examine the questions that support a life.

chapters.title: Loved or understood
chapters.description: Matthew distinguishes the need to be loved from the wish to be understood, and says he is most moved when student and teacher meet in open Dharma rather than role.

title: Talk: Spiritual Friendship and Sila as Our Practice
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on how practice is shaped not just by private effort, but by spiritual friendship, Sangha, and the ways other people enter the mind. He moves from the Buddha’s teaching that spiritual friendship is the whole of the holy life into a close exploration of sila: ethical conduct as embodied care, self-respect, and a life with less regret. The talk also turns toward retreat, moral sanity in a difficult world, and the quiet ways relationship helps us see ourselves more clearly.
short_summary: A talk on spiritual friendship, Sangha, and sila as the relational ground of practice and moral clarity.

chapters.title: Spiritual friendship
chapters.description: He opens with the Buddha and Ananda exchange to argue that awakening is fundamentally relational, not a solo project.

chapters.title: Mind is permeable
chapters.description: Using the Dhammapada and everyday examples, he describes the mind as conditioned, open, and shaped by what we let in.

chapters.title: Sangha as support
chapters.description: He tells a teaching story about learning to appreciate Sangha as the care that holds practice together.

chapters.title: Warm hand to warm hand
chapters.description: Drawing on Ajahn Sucitto, he explores transmission, heart resonance, and how practice is learned through living contact.

chapters.title: Not escape from life
chapters.description: He returns to retreat and the pressures of the wider world, stressing that practice is not withdrawal but a way of entering life more fully.

chapters.title: Training through habits
chapters.description: With William James and a mentor’s correction about kindness versus niceness, he shows how ethical life is built through repeated habits and honest care.

chapters.title: Seeing ourselves clearly
chapters.description: He develops the theme that relationship reveals blind spots, helping us understand our impact, our suffering, and the roots of empathy.

chapters.title: Sila and non-regret
chapters.description: He closes by linking moral expansion to sila as a life of reverence, carefulness, and non-regret that leaves less trace behind.

title: Evening Reflections
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: In this short evening reflection, Matthew Brensilver invites a simple shift of orientation: to appreciate one’s own goodness without turning that into self-indulgence. He explores the tension around self-love, deserving, shame, and pride, and then points toward a more basic sense of worthiness that comes before those judgments. The talk closes by encouraging that felt sense of goodness to accompany the listener into the night, sleep, and dreams.
short_summary: A brief reflection on self-love, worthiness, and letting a sense of goodness carry into the night.

chapters.title: Appreciating Goodness
chapters.description: The talk opens by saying it is not self-indulgent to appreciate your own goodness, even briefly.

chapters.title: Loving Yourself
chapters.description: The reflection extends the same invitation into self-love and notes that this can feel unfamiliar or charged.

chapters.title: Beyond Debates
chapters.description: The speaker names the inner arguments about deserving and undeserving, along with the pull of pride and shame.

chapters.title: A Deeper Worthiness
chapters.description: Rather than staying with moral judgments, the talk points to a kind of worthiness that comes before deserving and undeserving.

chapters.title: Carry It Into Sleep
chapters.description: The practice becomes an evening blessing: let that goodness follow you into bed, into the mind, and into dreams.

chapters.title: Closing Thanks
chapters.description: The talk ends quietly with thanks.

title: Talk: Knowing, Not-Knowing, Selfing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores Dharma as a disciplined movement between knowing and not knowing: learning enough to see suffering clearly, while loosening the need to control experience with certainty. He connects self-knowledge to dialogue, sangha, and the willingness to let familiar identity become less solid. The talk gradually shifts from ideas about practice into a close examination of how selfing is built from habit, feeling tone, and subtle assumptions.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on how self-knowledge depends on both clear seeing and a release of certainty.

chapters.title: Knowing and Not Knowing
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces the talk as a dance between knowing and not knowing, and frames selfing as something shaped by grasping and ignorance.

chapters.title: Safety and Power
chapters.description: He develops knowing and not knowing as complementary qualities, showing how practice needs both precision and an openness that does not cover over mystery.

chapters.title: Dialogue and Inquiry
chapters.description: Using Agnes Callard, he argues that self-understanding requires other people, honest questioning, and the courage to unsettle fixed answers.

chapters.title: Narrative Fractures
chapters.description: He turns to the ways self-narratives break down, and how practice reveals that our summaries of ourselves are always incomplete.

chapters.title: Ignorance and Dukkha
chapters.description: Drawing on the Buddha's teachings, he links ignorance to unskillful qualities, the Four Noble Truths, and the need to understand conditioning in order to be free.

chapters.title: Learning What Matters
chapters.description: He contrasts ordinary learning with Dharma learning, quoting Yeats, Ajahn Chah, and the Dhammapada to show that practice means loosening the mind's grip on security and certainty.

chapters.title: Renunciation of Control
chapters.description: Brensilver describes how compulsive knowing is driven by fear, and how surrender is the dissolution of volitional energies rather than a strategy for getting somewhere.

chapters.title: The Two Selves
chapters.description: He distinguishes narrative and sensorial selfing, then shows how the self becomes more visible when the mind gets quiet and what was transparent begins to look opaque.

chapters.title: Listening Through Identity
chapters.description: With Nisargadatta and Ajahn Sucitto, he emphasizes deep listening to feeling tone, mood, and habit energies, ending with a gentler view of self and more compassion for its arising.

title: Evening Reflections - A Poem
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reads and reflects on a poem about impermanence, using a mountain retreat in New Mexico as the setting for a quiet confrontation with time, fragility, and change. The talk moves from the feel of austere landscape to a direct meditation on mortality, then toward a more spacious relationship to fear and grief. It ends with a simple emphasis on remembering, letting go, and allowing the warmth that can come with loss.
short_summary: A brief reflection on impermanence that moves from mountain landscape to mortality, fear, and the warmth of grief.

chapters.title: Mountain Austerity
chapters.description: He opens with the feel of a retreat landscape and the way beauty and severity sharpen attention.

chapters.title: A Poem Appears
chapters.description: Matthew names and introduces the poem that will shape the rest of the reflection.

chapters.title: Stability Crumbles
chapters.description: The poem's lines on instability and non-existence are read as a direct challenge to ordinary assumptions of permanence.

chapters.title: Fear Wakes Up
chapters.description: He turns to the bodily force of impermanence, linking it to a childhood memory of dusk and to ancestral fear.

chapters.title: Aliveness Opens
chapters.description: What first felt like fear becomes a larger sense of aliveness as change is seen more clearly.

chapters.title: Remembering and Letting Go
chapters.description: The talk closes with a simple invitation to remember impermanence, release clinging, and allow grief to be felt.

title: Talk: Whys and Hows of Effort - Energy & Bhava Tanha
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on effort as something steadied by trust, patience, and a clear sense of why we practice. He moves from the hidden way goodness accrues, to motivation rooted in benefit for others, to the tension between wholesome effort and craving for becoming. The talk closes by warning against spiritual striving and inviting a more receptive, willing relationship to practice.
short_summary: A grounded talk on effort, motivation, and how to practice without getting trapped in craving or self-construction.

chapters.title: Listening and trust
chapters.description: He opens by reflecting on how to listen to Dharma talks selectively, then turns to effort, patience, and trust as the mood that lets practice take root.

chapters.title: Practice that simply continues
chapters.description: Using Sharon Salzberg and Nietzsche, he describes practice as something we keep doing from faith in the path, even when results are not obvious.

chapters.title: Why make the effort
chapters.description: He asks what actually sustains effort, shifts from self-centered motivation to concern for others, and points to the way healing and love spill beyond the self.

chapters.title: Precious human life
chapters.description: He introduces the Tibetan contemplations, beginning with the preciousness and strangeness of being born at all as a source of reverence and motivation.

chapters.title: Against the stream
chapters.description: A long aside on cosmology gives way to the Buddhist point that conditioning pushes us toward habit, while practice asks for a critical stance and real energy to let go.

chapters.title: Open palm of release
chapters.description: He explores how effort becomes the energy to listen to our own wisdom, then turns toward receptivity, surrender, and willingness rather than willfulness.

chapters.title: Non-gaining practice
chapters.description: Suzuki Roshi's teaching on non-gaining practice frames the danger of spiritual ambition and the subtle forms of craving that hide inside progress.

chapters.title: Your own path
chapters.description: He ends with a brief silence and a closing reminder to take what is useful, leave the rest, and let the path feel like one's own even within the Buddhist tradition.

title: Dharmette: Fear at the Gateway of Stillness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver responds to a question about fear that arises as meditation deepens and stillness begins to gather. He describes why letting go can feel unsafe, how vigilance and self-protection become fused with consciousness, and why the edge of practice can bring spiritual vertigo. The talk moves toward practical ways of meeting fear: knowing it clearly, softening it with love, acclimatizing to mortality and change, and trusting the silence rather than resisting it.
short_summary: A brief Dharma talk on fear that appears at the threshold of stillness, and how practice can meet it with clarity, love, and trust.

chapters.title: The question of fear
chapters.description: Matthew introduces a listener's question about fear arising in meditation and names the basic problem.

chapters.title: Safety and vigilance
chapters.description: He explores how survival conditioning, caretaking pressure, and cultural expectations can make vigilance feel necessary.

chapters.title: Why letting go feels unsafe
chapters.description: The talk turns to the way clinging, thinking, and worrying can feel like safety when uncertainty is present.

chapters.title: At the gateway
chapters.description: Matthew describes how deeper stillness can trigger the sense that one must do something, even though willfulness only adds contraction.

chapters.title: Spiritual vertigo
chapters.description: He names the jarring quality of attenuated self-states and the feeling of standing near an abyss as the ordinary self thins out.

chapters.title: Working with fear
chapters.description: Using the image of being 'on the cusp,' he suggests releasing the need for something to happen and making fear an object of clear awareness.

chapters.title: Training the nervous system
chapters.description: He notes that practice is often gradual, aimed at de-electrifying the past so old pain no longer ambushes us.

chapters.title: Mortality and faith
chapters.description: With references to Ajahn Sucitto and Suzuki Roshi, he shifts from hidden monsters to mortality, change, and the possibility of faith.

chapters.title: Surrender to silence
chapters.description: The talk ends by inviting trust in Dharma and lineage, and by framing silence as something that beckons rather than threatens.

title: Dharmette: Moral outrage and love
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on how deep care can become entangled with outrage, disgust, and the urge to punish. He asks how moral commitment can stay clear and useful without hardening into hatred, drawing on Ruth King and Hannah Chapman along the way. The talk moves toward grief, fear, and the idea that love—not anger—has to have the last word.
short_summary: A quiet reflection on moral outrage, disgust, fear, and how to keep love at the center of ethical action.

chapters.title: Love before practice
chapters.description: He begins with the depth of care already present in ordinary life and suggests that practice can widen and free that love.

chapters.title: Outrage and entanglement
chapters.description: The talk turns to moral outrage, the question of whether it can be trusted, and Ruth King’s reminder that anger can begin action but not complete it.

chapters.title: When care turns fierce
chapters.description: He explores how love can become yoked with rage, punishment, and revulsion, and asks how to use moral intensity without corroding the heart.

chapters.title: Purifying moral emotion
chapters.description: Drawing on Ruth King and his own uncertainty, he distinguishes useful engagement from the distortions of clinging, hatred, and the wish to force change.

chapters.title: Disgust and hate
chapters.description: A cited passage from Hannah Chapman helps frame moral disgust as a borrowed, ancient withdrawal response that can blur into hatred.

chapters.title: Fear and self-view
chapters.description: He emphasizes learning to surf strong moral emotions, take care of fear, and notice when moral commitment becomes egoic tribalism.

chapters.title: Love without a self
chapters.description: The talk deepens into self-view, empty processes, and the tendency to attach hatred to identity markers rather than seeing clearly.

chapters.title: Grief as the burden
chapters.description: He closes by saying moral sanity is kept through love, even if that means more grief, and that tears can become a source of motivation.

title: Dharmette: On not understanding dharma talks
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver responds to a listener who sometimes understands Dharma talks immediately and sometimes feels left out of them. He reflects on how teaching always involves judgment calls, why recognition can feel like remembering, and why not understanding can itself be part of wisdom. The talk gradually shifts toward patience, humility, and a practice of learning without needing to be right.
short_summary: A quiet reflection on why Dharma talks are not always immediately clear, and how not knowing can be part of practice.

chapters.title: The listener's question
chapters.description: Matthew reads the anonymous question about talks that feel clear versus talks that seem out of reach.

chapters.title: Teaching is imperfect
chapters.description: He reflects on the difficulty of teaching Dharma at all and the many editorial choices teachers make.

chapters.title: Recognition and intimacy
chapters.description: He turns to sincerity, the feeling of remembering, and the intimacy that can arise when a teacher speaks plainly to one's mind.

chapters.title: Wisdom in not knowing
chapters.description: Matthew says that recognizing what one does not understand is itself wise, and uses Agnes Callard to frame the challenge of questioning our own assumptions.

chapters.title: Learning to be wrong
chapters.description: He develops the theme of doubt, overconfidence, and the willingness to be surprised, drawing on Socrates as an example.

chapters.title: Practice matures
chapters.description: Matthew looks back on years of practice, describing a shift from dramatic discovery to a steadier but still vulnerable relation to Dharma.

chapters.title: Patience and belief
chapters.description: He ends by encouraging patience, saying there are no makeup classes for wisdom and that understanding often comes later than trust.

title: Equanimity 6
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver describes equanimity as a real practice that first takes effort and then, at times, opens into a much deeper letting go. He distinguishes ordinary balance from the Mahasi-style stage of “equanimity with formations,” where experience feels less sticky, the three characteristics are vivid, and the sense of self loosens into space and groundlessness. Toward the end, he pauses on the risks of turning insight into a state to chase, and returns to the practical question of how this changes a life of commitments and compassion.
short_summary: A grounded talk on equanimity as effortful cultivation, deep non-grasping insight, and the caution not to fetishize states.

chapters.title: Cultivating Equanimity
chapters.description: He begins by distinguishing the gradual training of equanimity from a more ripened expression of it, emphasizing the work and energy the practice can require.

chapters.title: Beyond Forcing Letting Go
chapters.description: Matthew describes how practice can move from wrestling with pleasant and unpleasant toward a quieter state where letting go is no longer forced.

chapters.title: Mahasi Equanimity
chapters.description: He explains the Mahasi tradition’s account of equanimity with formations, where awareness seems to observe itself and the mind becomes extremely clear.

chapters.title: The Three Characteristics
chapters.description: The talk turns to how dukkha, impermanence, and not-self appear vividly in this phase, with experience feeling more neutral, coreless, and less personal.

chapters.title: Safety In Groundlessness
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on the relief and security found when nothing sticks to the mind, while also noting how this can resemble dissociation if it goes in the wrong direction.

chapters.title: Rumi And Letting Go
chapters.description: He uses Rumi’s language of living in the nowhere you come from to point toward equanimity, then cites the Buddha on safety being found in relinquishment.

chapters.title: Compassion And Nihilism
chapters.description: He considers how this kind of release can deepen compassion rather than nihilism, and how it changes one’s relation to the drama of suffering.

chapters.title: Not Chasing States
chapters.description: Drawing on Shinzen Young, Matthew warns against making spiritual practice into a quest for special experiences and says mature practice becomes less bound up with states.

chapters.title: A Dharma Life
chapters.description: He closes by admitting it is hard to trace exactly what causes freedom, then lands on the sense that practice becomes an ongoing Dharma life.

title: Equanimity 5
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on why spiritual practice rarely fits a neat personal story, then turns that same humility toward equanimity: how it is noticed, strengthened, and limited in ordinary life. He connects equanimity with not-self, the draining of egoic charge, and the practical need to respect when a mind is too depleted to do much more than stop and regroup. The talk closes by widening equanimity into a care for others, where holding pain becomes part of love, solidarity, and remorse.
short_summary: A grounded talk on equanimity as something practiced through humility, limits, and care for others.

chapters.title: Unreliable spiritual stories
chapters.description: Brensilver questions the neat stories people tell about their own practice and why they cannot fully explain a life of Dharma practice.

chapters.title: Sincerity over certainty
chapters.description: He briefly invokes Suzuki Roshi to suggest that sincerity matters more than a clean yes-or-no idea of enlightenment.

chapters.title: Noticing lack of equanimity
chapters.description: He shifts to equanimity as something you can notice when you are caught, much as you notice a thought or trance.

chapters.title: Equanimity and not-self
chapters.description: The talk develops the mutual relationship between equanimity and not-self, especially through loosening the emotional charge around self-stories.

chapters.title: Draining self-charges
chapters.description: He gives everyday examples of letting identity stories rattle until their affect drains out, making the heart less brittle and reactive.

chapters.title: Respecting limits
chapters.description: Brensilver emphasizes practical limits: when the mind is gripped or depleted, the skillful move may simply be to stop, rest, or say no.

chapters.title: Expanding pain tolerance
chapters.description: He describes practice as gradually increasing tolerance for pain and reducing its unpleasantness, rather than eliminating sensation itself.

chapters.title: Holding pain for others
chapters.description: The talk ends by linking equanimity to compassion, solidarity, and the wish to be safe for others when our own regulation falters.

title: Equanimity 4
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a short, practice-oriented talk on equanimity as a companion to compassion rather than a dry or distant state. He begins with an alternating metta and equanimity exercise, then develops the theme through the experience of loving when we cannot control outcomes. The talk closes by bringing the teaching into everyday and civic life: where care is best spent, and how to remain open without over-identifying with suffering.
short_summary: A grounded reflection on how compassion and equanimity balance one another when care meets powerlessness.

chapters.title: Opening practice frame
chapters.description: He introduces walking practice and an alternating heart practice that moves between metta and equanimity.

chapters.title: Love at the limit
chapters.description: The talk turns to what love feels like when our wishes do not govern outcomes and control falls away.

chapters.title: Holding suffering lightly
chapters.description: He describes feeling another's suffering, letting grief be felt, and then softening into a wider, more relieving heart.

chapters.title: Two sides of love
chapters.description: Compassion and equanimity are presented as mutually supporting, with each correcting the distortions of the other.

chapters.title: Suffering wisely
chapters.description: He speaks about global dukkha, the danger of absorbing it in ways that corrode the heart, and the need to rest.

chapters.title: Local and practical care
chapters.description: A story about a politically engaged teacher leads into a suggestion to ask where love is most effective and local.

chapters.title: Closing practice instructions
chapters.description: He returns to the metta sequence, pairing taking on suffering with the 'as if' boundary and then releasing into equanimity.

title: Equanimity 3
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a careful, practical talk on equanimity as a way of meeting pleasant and unpleasant experience without bracing, suppression, or over-identification. He moves from broad reflections on how suffering shapes our narratives into a more detailed account of sensory balance, then brings in several contemporary and traditional teachers to sharpen the definition. The second half turns more inward and practical, with questions about difficult emotions, shame, jealousy, and the slow ripening of practice over time.
short_summary: A grounded exploration of equanimity as balanced, intimate contact with experience, especially in the face of unpleasantness and difficult emotion.

chapters.title: Beginning with affect
chapters.description: Matthew starts with the raw fact that much of our suffering and social narration grows out of how we meet pleasant and unpleasant feeling.

chapters.title: What equanimity means
chapters.description: He lays out several definitions of equanimity, distinguishing it from numbness and showing how it widens perspective.

chapters.title: Not self-absorption
chapters.description: The talk turns to how suffering narrows us and how equanimity can soften the compulsion to fix, tweak, or brace against experience.

chapters.title: A workable sequence
chapters.description: Using Alyssa Mrazek’s four aspects of acceptance, he breaks the practice into acknowledging, allowing, non-judgment, and non-attachment.

chapters.title: What it is not
chapters.description: He warns against over-reading moments of experience, and clarifies that equanimity is not apathy, passivity, or tolerance of injustice.

chapters.title: Compassion with balance
chapters.description: Matthew shows how equanimity supports more effective compassion by reducing compulsivity, desperation, and the urge to force change.

chapters.title: Love and gratitude
chapters.description: In dialogue with the room, he describes radical permission as a form of love and cites Stephen Levine on acceptance as gratitude.

chapters.title: How it ripens
chapters.description: He answers a question about conditions for equanimity by pointing to causes and effects in the awakening factors, repeated practice, and the mind being gently kneaded by practice.

chapters.title: Shame, mortality, and support
chapters.description: The closing questions move into shame, jealousy, and other hard zones, ending with the need for patience, small openings, and sometimes the steady gaze of a spiritual friend.

title: Equanimity 2
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores equanimity as a way of meeting experience without turning every sensation into a problem to solve. He traces how the mind reads events through self-relevance, how pleasant and unpleasant feelings trigger strategy and control, and why relaxing the impulse to manage each moment can reveal a simpler flow of arising and passing. The talk closes with a repeated settling into letting go, making it especially useful for listeners interested in how equanimity works in practice, not as indifference but as a softer relationship to feeling.
short_summary: A quiet talk on how self-relevance, alarm, and control shape suffering, and how equanimity can mean relaxing into the changing texture of experience.

chapters.title: Self-Relevance
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces how phenomena are evaluated through their significance to the project of the self.

chapters.title: Pleasant and Unpleasant
chapters.description: He describes how experiences feel pleasant when they support the project of life and unpleasant when they threaten it.

chapters.title: Alarm and Strategy
chapters.description: The talk turns to how alarm makes the present into a problem, pushing the mind toward control and disengagement from direct feeling.

chapters.title: What Equanimity Means
chapters.description: He reframes equanimity as dissolving the need to solve everything, allowing experience to be seen as arising and passing.

chapters.title: Desperation Around Pain
chapters.description: Brensilver names the panic that often arises with unpleasantness and distinguishes bodily pain from the larger prediction of ruin.

chapters.title: Relaxing the Bracing
chapters.description: He invites a relaxation of judgment, contraction, and the urgency to govern life moment by moment.

chapters.title: Letting Go Practice
chapters.description: The talk closes with a sustained repetition of letting go, emphasizing the felt release rather than a conceptual conclusion.

title: Equanimity 1
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores equanimity as something more active and intimate than calm or passivity: a way of meeting unpleasantness, helplessness, and feeling without trying to control experience. He moves from examples in retreat life, parenting, politics, and prison work to a practical claim that equanimity softens reactivity, reduces the pressure of preference, and supports wiser action. The talk includes short audience reflections and a long guided sitting that turns toward bodily sensation, models of self and world, and the felt sense of urgency.
short_summary: A quiet, wide-ranging talk on equanimity as relaxed, non-controlling contact with experience and feeling.

chapters.title: What retreat teaches
chapters.description: Matthew opens by contrasting lecture with the experiential work of retreat and frames the day as practice with the problem of suffering.

chapters.title: First meanings of equanimity
chapters.description: He introduces equanimity through Michelle McDonald’s phrase and begins distinguishing it from preference, grit, and emotional flattening.

chapters.title: Softening instead of freezing
chapters.description: The talk develops equanimity as a heart that softens in the face of imperfection rather than hardening into grim endurance.

chapters.title: Helplessness and wise action
chapters.description: Using questions from participants, including prison work, politics, and family life, he explores equanimity as love in the face of helplessness and as a basis for response rather than reaction.

chapters.title: Sitting with experience
chapters.description: Matthew leads a long period of guided silence, inviting attention to the body, the room, and the models we build of self, world, and future.

chapters.title: Friction falls away
chapters.description: He returns to the theme of equanimity as a reduction in compulsive friction, especially when the mind no longer waits for pleasure to rescue it.

chapters.title: Love it, not escape it
chapters.description: A playful dialogue with the Buddha and a quotation from the Satipatthana Sutta sharpen the claim that mindfulness begins by setting down covetousness and displeasure.

chapters.title: Feeling at the center
chapters.description: Matthew closes by linking equanimity to de-reification, the move from story to contour, and the pervasive role of feeling tone in every moment of mind.

title: Dharmette: The mirage of self evaporates as the fires cool
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver opens with a quote from Ajahn Sucitto and uses it to explore nibbana as cooling, spaciousness, and the easing of compulsive selfing. He moves between Buddhist teaching, reflections on lay practice, and a few outside references to show why liberation is less about improving the self than about letting the fires of craving and fear settle. The talk ends by turning toward a quieter, less territorial sense of freedom that leaves more room for care.
short_summary: A reflective talk on nibbana as cooling, spaciousness, and the softening of self-view through practice.

chapters.title: Ajahn Sucitto's frame
chapters.description: Matthew introduces Ajahn Sucitto's teaching on dispassion and cooling as the lens for the whole talk.

chapters.title: Practice turns inward
chapters.description: He reflects on how radical the path feels, especially for lay practitioners, and questions the wish to keep some of both samsara and nibbana.

chapters.title: Mara and conditioning
chapters.description: Using a brief Metzinger reference, he describes evolutionary conditioning, the reward system, and the self-model as forces that feed compulsivity.

chapters.title: Openness instead of denial
chapters.description: Matthew argues for respectful, radical openness to suffering rather than fighting or denying Mara, and names suffering as a kind of forgetting.

chapters.title: Space and cooling
chapters.description: He returns to nibbana as space in the mind and contrasts suffering's claustrophobia and heat with dispassion and tranquility that remain fully alive.

chapters.title: The self cannot own freedom
chapters.description: The talk turns to ego's tendency to colonize even spiritual practice, and then to the idea that freedom is subtraction, not an attainment.

chapters.title: What remains
chapters.description: With the image of the self as a hologram, he suggests that genuine dissolution of self leads to more care and a more open relation to life.

title: Dharmette: Remembering Ann Buck
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet remembrance of Ann Buck, beginning with a morning scene, a Dylan lyric, and the feeling of mortality that opened the talk. From there he moves through memories of Ann’s practice life, her fierceness and warmth, and the way her presence made Dharma feel ordinary and real. The talk ends by widening from personal grief to a meditation on how the living might honor the dead through memory, steadiness, and continued love.
short_summary: A memorial Dharma talk remembering Ann Buck through personal stories, practice reflections, and a closing poem on grief and memory.

chapters.title: Morning, Dylan, mortality
chapters.description: Matthew begins with a morning stoplight, a Dylan lyric, and the mood of mortality that frames the rest of the talk.

chapters.title: Ann’s passing and example
chapters.description: He introduces Ann Buck’s death, her long practice, and the strange coexistence of tragedy, humor, and perspective.

chapters.title: Life in the ordinary
chapters.description: A sequence of small, vivid moments shows how practice meets daily life, from coffee to a bright pink bike.

chapters.title: Cling, then let go
chapters.description: Matthew recalls retreat memories with Ann and a shared recognition of attachment and release.

chapters.title: Her direct kindness
chapters.description: He describes Ann’s fierce clarity, including her strong encouragement to love oneself, and the trust that came with her words.

chapters.title: Dharma through conversation
chapters.description: Their phone calls and shared conversations are presented as naturally infused with Dharma and grounded support.

chapters.title: Mind, aging, and refuge
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on Ann’s practice lineage and the steadiness of a mind described as empty, radiant, and responsive.

chapters.title: What the dead might want
chapters.description: He closes with a James Fenton poem that turns grief toward memory, generosity, and continued life.

title: Dharmette: To Care and Not to Care Simultaneously
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores T.S. Eliot’s phrase “teach us to care and not to care” as a lived Dharma question, not a slogan. He moves from the alternating rhythms of compassion and rest to a more radical possibility: caring and not caring at the same time, as an expression of emptiness and release. The talk then turns to sutta imagery of perception thinning out and consciousness no longer landing anywhere, ending with a quiet suggestion that this freedom may still feel benevolent and warm.
short_summary: A reflection on balancing compassion and equanimity, leading into emptiness as simultaneous care and non-attachment.

chapters.title: Eliot and the question
chapters.description: Matthew opens with T.S. Eliot’s Ash Wednesday and uses its line about caring and not caring to frame the talk’s central tension.

chapters.title: Love and rest
chapters.description: He describes care as costly and argues for a rhythm between compassion and rest, rather than a heart that is either closed or overextended.

chapters.title: Beyond sequence
chapters.description: The talk shifts from alternating states to the possibility of caring and not caring simultaneously, which he presents as one way emptiness can be understood.

chapters.title: Refining perception
chapters.description: Using the Lesser Discourse on Emptiness, he traces perception becoming less agitated, from village to wilderness to earth, with fewer coarse forms appearing.

chapters.title: Less and less
chapters.description: He extends the emptiness teaching into subtler states of perception, including the sense of infinite space and a mind less dependent on ordinary sensory life.

chapters.title: Where mind does not land
chapters.description: Matthew turns to the sunlight simile from another sutta to describe consciousness that does not land anywhere, connecting that freedom with the end of sorrow.

chapters.title: Quiet closing
chapters.description: He offers the teaching for reflection, then closes with a brief farewell and well-wishing.

title: Dharmette: Unsustainable Trajectories & Love
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver begins with the Buddha’s recognition that a life organized around pleasure was unsustainable, then widens that lens to social, technological, and ecological conditions that can also feel unstable. He sits with fear, anger, and the pull toward nihilism, but keeps returning to a steadier response: practice, hope stretched across longer time scales, and a commitment to love, non-harming, and compassion. The talk closes with a local story about a rescued goose on Mount Shasta, which becomes a small but affecting image of care and spiritual friendship.
short_summary: A reflection on unsustainable conditions, the limits of hope and fear, and a return to love, practice, and care.

chapters.title: Unsustainable beginnings
chapters.description: Matthew starts with the Buddha’s realization that his own way of life could not last and draws a parallel to the instability people feel now.

chapters.title: A larger trajectory
chapters.description: He expands the idea of unsustainability from one life to society, technology, inequality, and climate.

chapters.title: Anger and confusion
chapters.description: The talk turns toward evidence, overconfidence, and the way anger can simplify the world into agents and causes.

chapters.title: Powerlessness and nihilism
chapters.description: Matthew names the emotional strain of the moment, including the temptation to collapse into futility or escape.

chapters.title: Freedom and hope
chapters.description: He contrasts freedom independent of conditions with the need, at times, to keep hope alive without clinging to the wrong things.

chapters.title: Planting long seeds
chapters.description: Hope is widened beyond a single lifetime, and love is framed as patient seed-planting rather than immediate rescue.

chapters.title: Back to fundamentals
chapters.description: He returns to the Dhammapada’s simple instruction to do no evil, do what is skillful, and purify the mind.

chapters.title: The goose on Shasta
chapters.description: A rescued goose becomes a gentle story of effort, care, tenderness, and the necessity of stewardship.

title: Dharmette: Truth & Lies in the US in 2025
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on truthfulness as a Dharma practice, starting from his childhood anger at hypocrisy and moving into the political atmosphere of the United States in 2025. He distinguishes lying from “bullshit,” connects honesty to care for others, and argues that both self-deception and social deception weaken our capacity to see clearly. The talk closes with practical invitations: treasure truth, give up dependence on the world honoring it, and find healing in sangha, where reality does not need to be denied.
short_summary: A Dharma reflection on truthfulness, self-deception, and staying sane in a culture of political dishonesty.

chapters.title: Hypocrisy and early anger
chapters.description: He begins with a childhood sensitivity to hypocrisy and the feeling of being mobilized by lies and denial.

chapters.title: Honesty as moral care
chapters.description: He links truth-telling to empathy, trauma recovery, and the moral responsibility to help others know what is real.

chapters.title: Lies and bullshit
chapters.description: He turns to Buddhist ethics and Harry Frankfurt to distinguish deliberate lying from indifference to truth.

chapters.title: Truth in practice
chapters.description: He uses Thomas Metzinger to connect scientific rigor and spiritual practice through a shared commitment to veracity.

chapters.title: Political outrage cycle
chapters.description: He names the current U.S. political moment and describes how anger, evidence-gathering, and self-righteousness can become a closed loop.

chapters.title: Treasure the truth
chapters.description: He offers a practical shift: speak truth as best one can, stop depending on the world to reward truthfulness, and give up the debate.

chapters.title: Seeing self-deception
chapters.description: He reflects on motivated reasoning and Nietzsche's line about truth and strength, turning it into a wish to become more able to bear truth.

chapters.title: Sangha and letting go
chapters.description: He ends by naming sangha as a place where reality can be faced together and by reminding listeners to let go before clinging is broken.

title: Dharmette: more of our personality in freedom than in our suffering
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on a reversal of Tolstoy’s famous line: suffering can feel intensely personal, but freedom may reveal more of our actual uniqueness. Drawing on Buddhist teaching, Carl Rogers, Winnicott, and Bruce Tift, he explores how empathy, relinquishment, and reduced compulsivity allow a more spontaneous, distinctive self to appear. The talk stays grounded in the felt contrast between the performative quality of suffering and the quiet freshness of liberation.
short_summary: A reflection on how suffering feels singular, while freedom expresses our deeper individuality.

chapters.title: Tolstoy Reversed
chapters.description: He opens with Tolstoy and turns the famous line around to show that suffering may be more alike across people than it first appears.

chapters.title: Pain Feels Unique
chapters.description: He lingers on how suffering can feel like a private brand, while also pointing to its shared, disconnecting character.

chapters.title: Meeting Others Carefully
chapters.description: Using Carl Rogers, he describes empathic attunement as entering another’s world without losing perspective, and notes that another person’s freedom can feel surprising and instructive.

chapters.title: What Freedom Looks Like
chapters.description: He turns to the Buddha’s emphasis on cessation and asks what a life freed from greed, hate, and delusion might actually look like in ordinary personality and ethics.

chapters.title: True Self and Gesture
chapters.description: With Winnicott, he contrasts role-playing and melodrama with spontaneous gesture, suggesting that freedom lets deeper particularity come forward.

chapters.title: Already Enlightened
chapters.description: He quotes Bruce Tift on treating people as already enlightened and uses that line to reframe how we see ourselves and others.

chapters.title: Closing Questions
chapters.description: He ends by inviting reflection on how practice reveals both our own freedom and the uniqueness of others.

title: Attention and Anicca, Awareness and Anatta
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores the difference between attention and awareness, using humor, retreat stories, and quoted teachers to show how practice changes the way experience is known. He moves from attention as a form of love and prayer into the ways attention reveals impermanence, selfing, and the limits of control. The talk gradually turns toward awareness as something dependently arisen and not owned by a self, ending with a quiet emphasis on surrender, non-identification, and the simplicity of knowing.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on attention, awareness, impermanence, and the softening of self through practice.

chapters.title: From Fixing to Listening
chapters.description: Matthew opens with a comic story about trying to solve a partner’s problems before turning to the simpler discipline of truly listening.

chapters.title: Attention as Love
chapters.description: He links listening, meditation, and love, then introduces Simone Weil’s idea that attention can function like prayer.

chapters.title: Unmixed Attention
chapters.description: Using Weil more fully, he describes attention purified of craving and selfing as something that sanctifies ordinary experience.

chapters.title: Right Now, Like This
chapters.description: Ajahn Sumedho’s phrase and reflections on conscious experience lead into a contrast between what we feel and the deeper currents beneath awareness.

chapters.title: What Awareness Means
chapters.description: A story about asking Shinzen Young what awareness is opens a discussion of attention, awareness, and the different emphases of Buddhist lineages.

chapters.title: How Attention Shapes Experience
chapters.description: He draws on the Buddha, William James, and attention science to describe attention as the condition through which phenomena, concentration, and training arise.

chapters.title: Attention and Impermanence
chapters.description: The talk turns to anicca, the costs of effortful practice, and the way attention can strengthen the sense of an agent at the center of experience.

chapters.title: Awareness Without Control
chapters.description: A child’s cry in the night leads into a gentler account of awareness as dependent on conditions rather than personal will, and of love as seeing the three characteristics clearly.

chapters.title: Surrendering the Center
chapters.description: The close shifts into a deeper relinquishment of the narrative self, with a philosophical note on silent, non-conceptual awareness and the innocence of self-reappearance.

title: Dharmette: It's Complicated and It's Simple
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver opens by dwelling on the sheer complexity of mind, life, and the systems we live within, drawing on neuroscience and cosmology to suggest that much of existence remains beyond our full grasp. He then turns toward a quieter frame: a retreatant’s simple insight that life may come down to love and heartbreak, and the Buddha’s deeper distinction between suffering and peace. The talk settles into a humane invitation to love what is here, let the heart break, and meet that with care and wisdom rather than confusion.
short_summary: A reflection on the tension between life’s complexity and the simpler truth of love, heartbreak, suffering, and peace.

chapters.title: Life Is Complicated
chapters.description: He begins with the complexity of the world, our lives, and the systems we inhabit, setting up the talk’s opening tension.

chapters.title: Brains and Networks
chapters.description: Matthew uses Wolf Singer’s remarks to show how brain science keeps becoming more abstract and relational rather than simpler.

chapters.title: Stories We Tell
chapters.description: He turns to how we explain ourselves, noting that our stories about motives and identity may not match the actual causes of our actions.

chapters.title: The Reach of the Cosmos
chapters.description: A brief cosmology passage widens the frame further, ending with the sense that there is much we will never know.

chapters.title: Love and Heartbreak
chapters.description: A retreat student’s simple insight becomes the bridge from complexity to a more direct understanding of experience.

chapters.title: Right Distinctions
chapters.description: Matthew invokes the Buddha’s teaching that confusion comes from the wrong categories, while practice points toward suffering and peace.

chapters.title: No Cure for Love
chapters.description: Using Leonard Cohen, he reflects on love’s inevitability and heartbreak’s inseparability, especially in close relationships.

chapters.title: Yielding to Time
chapters.description: He closes by naming the need to let life be offered back to time, holding both aspiration and tenderness without losing sight of either.

title: Metta for Self
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a talk and guided practice on metta for oneself, beginning with a broad framing of love as something that makes human life bearable. He distinguishes the four Brahma-viharas as different species of love, then spends much of the talk showing how self-mettta can work as cultivation, concentration, and a way of meeting whatever pain arises. The practice settles into repeated phrases of blessing and a steady invitation to see oneself through kind, merciful eyes, ending with a sense of love as vast and measureless.
short_summary: A talk and guided practice on self-directed loving-kindness that treats metta as cultivation, concentration, and a way of meeting hidden pain.

chapters.title: Love and human life
chapters.description: He opens by saying he wants to talk about love, then frames love as what makes the human situation bearable.

chapters.title: Four kinds of love
chapters.description: He names the Brahma-viharas as four species of love and distinguishes metta, compassion, joy, and equanimity.

chapters.title: How metta works
chapters.description: He explains metta as cultivation and as a radical sensitization to goodness, especially when the practice becomes quiet and focused.

chapters.title: What metta reveals
chapters.description: He describes how loving-kindness can also pull grief, rage, numbness, or fear to the surface, and suggests meeting that material with kindness.

chapters.title: Finding your own path
chapters.description: He encourages each person to find a native, sustainable way into love, then begins turning toward direct self-directed practice.

chapters.title: A merciful gaze
chapters.description: He invites listeners to see themselves through kind eyes and imagine how a deeply loving person would look at them.

chapters.title: Blessing the self
chapters.description: He brings in the example of a teacher's compassionate gaze, then moves into repeated phrases of wishing safety, ease, and happiness for oneself.

chapters.title: Problems dissolving
chapters.description: He emphasizes that loving-kindness does not solve problems so much as soften them, and repeats that the burden of 'my life' can be put down.

chapters.title: What has not been loved
chapters.description: He returns to the parts of experience that have not yet been loved and closes with a note that love makes the mind vast and measureless.

title: Moral Elevation, Moral Disgust and the Vastness Required to Open to Dukkha
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores how Dharma practice can sensitize us to goodness without losing contact with suffering. He moves from moral elevation and the force of ordinary courage into moral disgust, asking how care can stay clear of hatred, cynicism, and political reactivity. The talk gradually widens into a reflection on vastness, mortality, and impermanence, ending with Ajahn Chah’s tea cup as a way to hold life with gratitude and less fear.
short_summary: A reflection on goodness, moral disgust, and the need to widen the mind enough to meet suffering without turning to hatred.

chapters.title: Opening the Heart
chapters.description: Matthew introduces kama muta and moral elevation as ways the heart is moved by goodness.

chapters.title: Wesley Autry's Courage
chapters.description: He tells the subway rescue story and uses it to show how ordinary bravery can evoke and transmit moral beauty.

chapters.title: Sensitivity to Goodness
chapters.description: The talk turns toward metta, Rumi, and the way devotion can make goodness feel ordinary, embodied, and shareable.

chapters.title: Moral Disgust
chapters.description: Matthew contrasts moral elevation with moral disgust and warns that ethical feeling can be pulled toward aversion and brutality.

chapters.title: Prophetic Speech
chapters.description: Using Frederick Douglass and Heschel, he considers moral language that burns past ordinary conscience without collapsing into moralizing.

chapters.title: Distance from Rumination
chapters.description: He brings in self-distancing research to distinguish helpful reflection from looping pain and to suggest a wider perspective on suffering.

chapters.title: Widening the Horizon
chapters.description: The talk expands into global care, mortality, and Kore-eda's After Life as a glimpse of being less trapped by becoming.

chapters.title: Already Broken
chapters.description: Matthew ties cessation of becoming to the broken tea cup teaching and shows how impermanence can open the heart to love and gratitude.

title: Dharmette: Reflections on words from Ajahn Sucitto
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on a short teaching from Ajahn Sucitto and uses it to unfold a quiet map of the path: generosity, ethics, renunciation, discernment, persistence, and patience. He moves from the felt meaning of sharing to the practical question of how to direct energy, make choices, and time action well. The talk stays grounded in ordinary examples and in the sense that practice is less about intensity than about steady, responsive care.
short_summary: A reflection on Ajahn Sucitto’s teaching that traces generosity and the rest of the path as expressions of interdependence, ethical care, and patient discernment.

chapters.title: Reading the quote
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the Ajahn Sucitto passage and names the cluster of qualities it gathers together.

chapters.title: Generosity as interdependence
chapters.description: He expands generosity beyond material giving into an attitude shaped by relation, example, and shared blessing.

chapters.title: Sharing Dharma wisely
chapters.description: The talk turns to how advice lands, why example matters, and how generosity can be expressed without attachment to view.

chapters.title: Ethics and kindness
chapters.description: Matthew links interdependence with ethical choice, then pauses with a story and a study of random acts of kindness.

chapters.title: Morality and renunciation
chapters.description: He moves through self-respect, non-regret, and the question of enough, using personal anecdotes to show discernment in action.

chapters.title: Steady persistence
chapters.description: The emphasis shifts from peaks of effort to the daily steadiness that supports lasting change.

chapters.title: Patience and timing
chapters.description: Matthew closes with patience as sensitivity to ripeness, causality, and when a move is truly called for.

chapters.title: Closing reflections
chapters.description: He thanks Ajahn Sucitto, offers the teaching for consideration, and ends with a brief farewell.

title: Ill-Will & Metta
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores ill-will and metta as two very different ways the heart meets suffering. He moves from the mechanics of aversion—how craving, blame, and anger narrow experience—to the possibility of purification, radical accountability, and a softer, more spacious response. Near the end, he turns toward loving-kindness as a form of non-violence that can hold pain without collapse, and he closes by linking wisdom with love and non-contention.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on how aversion narrows experience, and how metta opens a more honest and compassionate way to meet suffering.

chapters.title: Opening the problem
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces ill-will and frames it as a familiar way of suffering that the talk will examine closely.

chapters.title: The Dharma as a broad remedy
chapters.description: He uses pharmacology to argue that Dharma works through many mechanisms, not just the surface sign of a calm mind.

chapters.title: Purification and practice
chapters.description: He distinguishes concentration from purification and says the path includes peaks, valleys, and whatever is present now.

chapters.title: What and how
chapters.description: The talk turns to the 'what' and the deeper 'how' of practice, showing how motivations shape what we seem to know.

chapters.title: Craving shapes the view
chapters.description: He explores how craving and aversion thicken experience, using his own childhood sensitivity to show how ill-will can become a strategy for managing pain.

chapters.title: Mara and the turning inward
chapters.description: Brensilver describes the habitual impulses to approach, avoid, or ignore, then shows how clinging and aversion reinforce helplessness and suffering.

chapters.title: The heat of aversion
chapters.description: Using the boiling-pot simile, he explains how ill-will obscures judgment, while Agnes Callard, Ruth King, and the Dalai Lama help refine the view of anger as partially functional but never transformative.

chapters.title: Facing and owning anger
chapters.description: He turns to noticing ill-will directly, describing radical accountability as a training that stops externalizing blame without denying external causes.

chapters.title: Metta as antidote
chapters.description: The emphasis shifts to metta as warm friendliness, free of possessiveness and control, and as a way of being moved by goodness and finding a home in it.

title: Wednesday Evening Reflection: Rohini's Enlightenment Poem
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on a line from Rohini’s enlightenment poem: the path is not a line on a map, but a great shining world, enter wherever you like. He uses that image to gently undo the pressure to fit Dharma practice into a fixed shape, suggesting instead that we discover our own belonging within it. The talk closes with an encouragement to affirm one’s efforts, rest well, and continue noticing how apparent walls may become doorways.
short_summary: A brief reflection on belonging in Dharma, centered on the image of the path as a great shining world.

chapters.title: A shining world
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the poem’s image of the path as a great shining world rather than a fixed route.

chapters.title: Not fitting in
chapters.description: He examines the tendency to feel we must mold ourselves to match Dharma or Dharma culture.

chapters.title: Enter anywhere
chapters.description: The teaching turns toward the freedom to begin practice from whatever conditions we actually have.

chapters.title: Belonging and home
chapters.description: He links belonging with a powerful human motivation and describes Dharma as something that can come to feel like home.

chapters.title: Nothing left behind
chapters.description: Matthew emphasizes that ultimately we are not forced to abandon any part of the heart in order to practice.

chapters.title: From every direction
chapters.description: He repeats the invitation to enter from anywhere, even when we feel awkward, remote, or contorted.

chapters.title: Affirming effort
chapters.description: The talk softens into encouragement to value one’s efforts without turning that recognition into self-absorption.

chapters.title: Rest and continue
chapters.description: He ends with a blessing to practice well, rest well, and keep going as new Dharma doors appear.
